data.title: This Is Why December Could Flip My Rating From Sell To Buy
data.description:
November is about to end largely unchanged from the previous month for the S&P 500. Yet, despite the appearance of a "resilient bull market," it was actually a volatile month, with mega-cap tech stocks under pressure, then recovering, and liquidity starting to run low in the system. In short, it was anything but a calm month.

In this analysis, we'll recap what happened during November and offer a view for December.

Growth slows, value picks up again

As I mentioned, from a distance, the S&P 500 (SP500) appears to have shown resilience. The Nasdaq is less able to hide its weakness, posting a loss of more than 2% in November, thus breaking a streak of consecutive positive months.

Growth vs. Value

The comparison between growth and value stocks is very clear and shows how growth stocks have significantly underperformed, primarily due to the technology sector. Value stocks, on the other hand, are regaining ground. This type of movement suggests that the index didn't move unilaterally/directionally, but rather that there was a genuine rotation during the month. Investors preferred to reduce risk and shift it to more defensive assets.

Personally, I don't believe the market has entered a new long-term paradigm, but the signal is there, it's clear, and it must be read and analyzed as such to better understand the underlying situation. In my opinion, it reflects an awareness I've been repeating for several weeks: if valuations are so high, a single low will be enough to bring everything down in one fell swoop. Typically, the onset of such rotations indicates that the market is beginning to doubt the validity of these valuations.

Sectors

In terms of sectors, the market's shift from "euphoria" to "caution" mode is even more evident, with sectors like healthcare, consumer staples, and materials performing better than sectors like information technology, consumer discretionary, and industrials, which performed poorly during the month.

Volatility

Crowding, especially in light of the mega-cap tech earnings and the Fed's comments, was evident when looking at the options market as a whole. Indeed, as you can see from the chart below, the put/call ratio first soared and then suddenly fell. Essentially, we went from a euphoric market, filled with call options being bought in droves, to a much more neutral position in just a few days. What I'd like to emphasize, however, is the speed of the movement. The​‍​‌‍​‍‌ options market was extremely out of balance before the mega-cap earnings and the Fed's comments; a matter of fact, the put-call skew had even reversed, which is an indication that investors were paying a higher premium for calls than for puts. To put it another way, basically all players were on the same side, believing that big tech companies would keep delivering good results. ​‍​‌‍​

Indeed, yes, earnings performed very well, with the exception of a few stocks that disappointed. Despite this, it took very little, combined with a less dovish Fed commentary than expected, and the excess deflated in two sessions.

From here, we can sense the markets' nervousness. We're now at more normal levels, but the market has provided us with a clear reading, and we must acknowledge it.

The problem isn't the Fed, but liquidity.

At first glance, it would seem that the Fed's comments have changed the market narrative. Or Nvidia (NVDA), with its doubts about its balance sheet (inventories and loans). Or the news of Google's Tensor Processing Units, or TPUs (GOOGL), which caused some AI stocks to collapse and supported others. There were no significant macro data (due to the shutdown), so I can confirm that the real move, the one that split the month in two and created a rotation of sectors and investment styles, was liquidity. It's always happened: when liquidity dwindles, problems begin to emerge.

As I've stated in some of my recent analyses, in recent weeks/months the TGA has reached around €1 trillion. This was caused by the shutdown, which allowed the government to accumulate liquidity in the Treasury account and avoid spending it on the real economy (wages, agricultural subsidies, etc.).

Added to this is the issue of the "Standing Repo Facility." Banks aren't using the repo facility. Which is actually abnormal. It was specifically created by the Fed with the sole purpose of providing liquidity to the system at negligible cost when needed; yet, banks prefer to turn to the money market, which tends to be more expensive. The question is: why? The answer is: they don't want to appear weak in the eyes of the Fed.

Add to this the fact that bank reserves (another figure no one looks at until it's too late) have fallen to levels that historically coincide with fragile market phases.

All of this data combined increases market stress and its sensitivity to any setback. So forget about the Fed, quarterly reports, and AI. These aren't the causes of this month's stress. They're the excuses the market has found to correct itself. Let's first look at how dollars move within the system, and then talk about valuations, the Nasdaq, AI, and whatnot. But as long as gas is scarce, we won't get very far.

A more mature AI?

Let's forget about the AI ​​we've been talking about for the last twelve months. The one focused on GPUs and the "without Nvidia, you can't do AI." That world is changing. When an industry stops being monocentric and expands. What we saw in November are the first real indication that AI is moving to phase two. There are new players, new metrics, a new AI economy. (I made a article about Alphabet; if you want to know, it is a good piece to be read). This deepening of competitiveness is very important not only for Alphabet but also for the whole AI ecosystem, and in particular for Nvidia.

Why do I say this? Because the AI ​​industry today faces a problem that is often ignored: capex is growing at an almost unsustainable rate, far faster than companies' ability to generate returns.

The following chart (capex vs. cash flow for hyperscalers) illustrates what I'm talking about. In ten years, we've gone from a "normal" ratio to a point where, by 2026, capital expenditures could absorb nearly 80% of the aggregate cash flow of Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Oracle. This isn't a situation that can last forever. It's financial stress. Of course, I'm also convinced that spending will somehow normalize sooner or later. But how? Maybe we've found the solution.

The real risk of AI isn't the bubble, but...

The risk isn't an "AI bubble." The risk is financial saturation. That's why spending needs to normalize and companies need to start cashing in. That's why Google, AWS, Microsoft, and Meta's entry with alternative chips to GPUs is crucial. Not because they'll "kill Nvidia" (spoiler: they won't), but because they lower system temperatures.

Let me be clear: I'm not saying TPUs are a valid alternative to GPUs. Absolutely not. Nvidia's chips are the most general-purpose, much more versatile, but also more expensive and, in some ways, more energy-hungry. Other manufacturers, however, focus on single-task chips, which are therefore less versatile but also less expensive. In recent versions, they have also managed to be much more energy efficient.

Google has been producing these chips for 10 years, but only recently have they come back into the spotlight, as many (including META) are considering using them instead of Nvidia's (or even diversifying) to use them in their own data centers.

"The American DeepSeek"

We can see Alphabet's TPUs as the American DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK). The first real system shock. They highlighted the fact that it is possible to create cutting-edge AI without buying only Nvidia GPUs. The most absurd fact (I call it the "AI paradox") is that this competition is primarily beneficial to Nvidia. That​‍​‌‍​‍‌ might sound quite ridiculous, but it is actually the fact. The industry dominated by Nvidia, on which everyone depends in one way or another, is way less stable than the one where Nvidia is the top player, however, there are other companies providing the same ​‍​‌‍​‍‌product.

  • If​‍​‌‍​‍‌ hyperscalers providers faces a decline in profits, which company will reduce its orders first? Nvidia.
  • If cash flow is not enough to meet expenses, which company is at the risk of not being paid on time? Nvidia.
  • If the cost of the industry increases significantly, which company is blamed for "stifling the AI ​​cycle"? ​‍​‌‍​‍‌Nvidia.

So, believe me: the​‍​‌‍​‍‌ risk is not competition. The risk is un-sustainability. A more competitive but stable market is preferable to a unmanageable monopoly.

Conclusion

As of this writing, the market appears to be positioning itself for a 25 basis point rate cut in December (80% probability). Unfortunately, the BLS will not release macroeconomic data before the FOMC meeting; these data will arrive later due to the shutdown. Also keep an eye on Oracle's quarterly results: they could provide more insight into the company's health and the health of the AI ​​sector overall.

Returning to the Fed, the surprise could be the return of QE. As I've said in the past, the Fed typically moves in three phases:

  1. Rate cuts
  2. End of QT
  3. Start of QE.

This is the cycle. We're in the second phase; only the third is missing to complete the picture. If it doesn't happen in December, it will happen in January, but it's something that will inevitably happen because it has become necessary. When the liquidity system tightens like this, the Fed has few options. It must intervene. And the only quick way is to buy Treasuries. Call it QE, call it what you will, but the essence is the same.

I​‍​‌‍​‍‌ will simply change my mind if the Federal Reserve restarts purchases even if it is just for a small amount. Without a second thought, I will move my rating from "Sell" to "Buy." It's a matter of oxygen. The market will be different if cash inflow starts ​‍​‌‍​‍‌again.

That's why I say December won't be a game of "what will Powell say on the microphone?" The real game will be what happens behind the scenes. If even a minimal easing occurs:

  • financing calms down,
  • reserves increase,
  • pressure on indices eases,
  • and the AI ​​theme can continue to grow without slowing down (or at least without a crash).

December could be the watershed moment we've all been waiting for. From a struggling market to a market that's finally starting to breathe again. If the Fed really does what I think, Santa's present will be under the tree.

Thanks for reading. If you like, feel free to leave a comment with your opinions on this market.

data.title: Siemens Energy: At An All-Time-High, My Rating Remains Hold
data.description:
If there's one flaw I'd ascribe to myself when it comes to investing, it's underestimating the potential for market irrationality, or how "good growth can get" in a company, even if the underlying earnings do not support it. I'm too much of a hardcore valuation investor to ignore facts and earnings. Sometimes this leads to exiting investments too early (relative to what they achieve), or underestimating reversal or growth potential in a company.

Siemens Energy AG (OTCPK:SMEGF, FWB: ENR) is a good example of this, especially what has happened to the company as of late. Since my last article, Siemens Energy is up almost 80%, against a market that's up around 30%. It's a clear outperformance. Part of it is certainly because the company has turned around things better and faster than expected, but I will continue to argue that a big part of it is continued exuberance.

In this article, I will attempt a balanced view and update for this company, to see what it could offer, and where it could go from here. But one thing's clear - if you are looking for someone to tag along to the next "growth story," even if that growth is exuberant, I'm not your guy. I'm more the investor who invests in things when they are dirt cheap, willing to hold for years until a turnaround.

When it comes to Siemens Energy, I exited early because at the time the stock was divested, it wasn't a good or appealing company.

That's something that has changed a bit.

The latest results were reported about 2 weeks ago, finalizing the 2025A fiscal. Because of excellent performance and fulfilling all goals, it's not surprising that we've seen impressive growth in the stock and, in a big way, not something that I had expected of the company, to be sure.

Most recommendations for the company here are positive. Let me show you why I'm raising my target and my expectations, but why I remain at least somewhat skeptical about the broadly-painted positive thesis we have here.

Siemens Energy Q4 '25 - Good results, good quarter, good forecasts - but at what price?

I could show you my problems with Siemens Energy in one slide or picture, but this would perhaps be too simplistic given the returns we're seeing. So I feel that I have to explain it a bit more.

Let's start with Q4 '25.

The company's full-year results fully justify an increased confidence, or upside, if you will. With solid order growth, record backlogs, margin growth of over 500 bps, and favorable pricing trends across the company, I can't find anything to criticize about this quarter - or indeed about the full year by the company. For the next year, the company is expecting yet another double-digit rate of growth. But what is most interesting is what the company expects in terms of margins. 6% in this year (4.5% in Q4) is expected to rise to, or above 10% in 2026, and to 15-16% in 2028E.

The company also finally introduced its dividend again. This was only a matter of time, if the company turned around, but given that it was 63% more than I expected, the company does get "points" for this. Still, the company's argument in Q4 '25 is that the share price rose over 200% in a year - and this is true.

But it's also 200% from a level of essentially very low, and the 500 bps margin increase - that was from a negative profit margin. So while I like what I see, I'm not as quick to laud the company for everything of this, because I recall selling before things turned down horribly in Siemens Energy some years ago. The stock was a loser for well over 3 years, and I don't regret selling that (even if this is the result), because during that time I've made good returns.

But I did miss out on "entering back in" - and that needs to be made clear.

In a sense, I am trying to be objective here. I'm not trying to take away things from the company, but I'm also not letting readers, or the company, forget the journey that brought the business here. Siemens Energy was not an impressive name for a long time.

Summarizing, the company saw growth in virtually all segments and regions, and there is little in the company's current KPI's that gives indications of trouble.

So why am I not raising the company to what some analysts would consider a well-deserved "Buy"? What about the guidance raise, and what about the consensus until then?

I argue that part of the reason for outperformance is not good 2025A results, but in fact lofty 2028E estimates - and when it comes to those, I am doubtful. These increases are, like much today, based on AI/tech demand, specifically increased demand for gas for things like data centers and the ongoing coal-to-gas transition, which is partially driven by these trends. There is no doubt in my mind that the company now deserves a far higher fair value than it currently has, based on my last article, in addition to the lifting of the dividend restriction by the German government in 2024.

Maybe it's also because I'm still a bit burned by the 2020-2023 period (though I sold at a profit early), with issues that were at the center of the company's valuation and issues for a long time. Bulls would now point to things like the modernization of the grid and the technologies associated with this (like coal-to-gas, as mentioned), which creates demand for Siemens Energy products. This is one of the reasons I'm raising the target.

We could also argue for data center upside - that's another reason I'm increasing my upside - at least somewhat. Management doesn't get points for turnaround from me - that was, in my view, something that should have been done far earlier. What this means is "normalization" for me, not premiumization. We're talking about a Siemens business. To me, that means that quality is a presumption, not an alternative for management.

So what problems do I see that cause me to stay conservative here to the degree that I am?

Just because the company has turned things around does not mean that risks are gone. And just because there's currently a high demand for power-intensive data centers based on AI needs does not mean that this increase is going to see linear or proportional growth. The efficiency of alternative models such as DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) could significantly reduce the demand for Siemens Energy products. Also, if anyone thinks Wind power issues are over, I would strongly disagree with that. Unforeseen repair costs for turbines are still here, and could increase due to faster product cycles - as I've pointed out before. Innovation in the segment is high (in terms of demand), because competition is high. And if the company turns back to losses or negative, further cash injections and cap raises could become necessary (though I admit that the last one is a remote risk).

So while I acknowledge the positives here, I'm not the type of analyst to shift everything about a company because of a few positives.

Let's see what valuation tells us here.

Valuation for Siemens Energy

Do you think that an energy company should trade at close to 60x P/E?

Because if you don't, then Siemens Energy might not be for you.

I say this because Siemens Energy's current average weighted valuation is over 58x P/E normalized.

But, you might say, both the company and analysts are expecting significant growth going forward. Just look at that - by 2028, earnings will be 4-5x the current AEPS level, and then the valuation won't even be 30 or 25x, if considered like that.

Sure - but can we really consider this as gospel or likely? The company expects it, for sure. Even analysts seem to in part be on board with this. But let's remember that historically, forecasting this company has been impossible. With a 10% margin of error, forecasting the company has been missed both negatively and positively 100% of the time over the last 5 years. So declaring that we "Know" what's going to happen in the next 3 with this sort of conviction is quite ambitious - and I wouldn't want to do so.

Sure, using a 42x P/E average for the 5-year and using current growth estimates implies a 100%+ RoR even from this level.

This is a BBB-rated company. I no longer believe the company to be "very risky," as before. This is because Gamesa is now, at least for the time being, solved. Profitability in 2024, better in 2025. Breakeven is possible for 2026E, or even higher. But the current valuation targets are entirely, in my view, disregarding the cyclical nature of the company.

Unlike other analysts, I do penalize management a bit for the issues and for how long they persisted in Gamesa, while at the same time acknowledging it was a tricky legacy to inherit.

My previous fair value of around €45/share with a price target, or PT, of €30 or below to make up for the risk I saw is here raised to a fair value of €60/share, with a PT of €45/share. The fair value target represents a 25-30x P/E average for the next few years, which, in my view, is the highest possible target I'd want to give the company.

Analyst averages for Siemens Energy here are telling of how the volatility is in the company. The low-end target from 23 analysts is €37/share, lower even than my previous fair value target. The high-end target is €170/share. I have not previously observed such a significant variation between high-end and low-end targets, and I think this expresses a significant uncertainty and volatility for the company's future.

Based on this, I don't consider it too conservative to only give a PT of €45 and a fair value of €60/share. I am not changing my rating from Hold.

Risks for Siemens Energy here are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

This one is a bit of a learning opportunity. I underestimated the earnings growth potential and recovery of Siemens Energy - but I also believe the market is significantly overestimating the impact of EPS growth for the next few years. I believe the next few years of EPS growth will be a lot less than is currently forecasted, owing to demand risk and volatility, as well as legacy wind power repair costs.

If the company were to actually manage this upside, then the resulting EPS growth and potential could imply more than 100% TSR potential - but I would, once again, view that with a bit of uncertainty.

I have my new share price target of €45/share for the company, translating to ADR pricing of $52/share. Keep in mind that the current state of FX means the company is very differently priced in USD than in EUR.

Thesis

  • The company was one of the more interesting plays in all of Europe, on a mix of legacy gas/power as well as a massive renewable operation, it seeks to pull from the public markets. After excessively positive reactions or overreactions, as I consider them, the market is not showing me an attractively priced Siemens Energy. This means I'm negative on the business prospects for this company, and would be more careful than before. I am upgrading my targets, but sticking to my previous target of Hold.
  • I'm also sticking to my Hold rating, and I'm not adding shares of Siemens Energy back to my portfolio. My price point goes up to €45/share, with a fair value target of €60, but I want to see what the company does in terms of recovery and other things going forward before I initiate a new position in the stock. At this point, I would also want a substantially lower price.
  • For those reasons, I'm going to stick to my conservative here, and I believe investors should consider doing the same - and this is my stance for 2026E.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them:

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. I give the company a Hold rating as of November 2025 with a PT of €45/share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kinross Gold: Don't Chase The Stock Here
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study. Operating costs refer to production cost of sales, not all-in sustaining costs.

Introduction

Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) is a multi-asset gold producer with three operations in the United States (Alaska, Nevada), and its other three operations in Mauritania, Chile, and Brazil. The company's largest two assets which contribute just shy of 60% of annual production are in Brazil and West Africa, with these being Paracatu and Tasiast. As for reserves, the company has ~21.9 million ounces in reserves, albeit at grades well below the 500,000+ ounce producer peer average (0.77 G/T of gold vs. ~1.90 G/T of gold).

This is primarily related to the Paracatu, that's been operating commercially since 1987 (but artisanal mining dates back over 300 years with placer gold found in the Paracatu region); is one of the largest assets globally by throughput at ~60 MTPA, but which is a large-scale lower-grade deposit. This significant lower-grade reserve base (~4.89 million ounces at 0.40 G/T of gold) drags down Kinross' average reserve grade, as does its lower-grade Fort Knox Mine in Alaska.

Following what has been a strong delivery year-to-date vs. guidance (~1.53 million ounces of gold produced at $1,490/oz AISC), Kinross has enjoyed a solid year-to-date performance, gaining over 190% year-to-date which complements an already impressive 50% return last year. That said, the stock has found itself running straight into a 30-year resistance level coupled with a short-term technical sell signal that severely increases the probability of rallies being sold into.

(*) Full-year 2025 guidance is ~2.0 million ounces at ~$1,500/oz AISC (*).

If we combine this with the fact that Kinross offers no real margin of safety at current levels, I see this as a dangerous point to be chasing the stock here above US$27.50. This is because Kinross is now trading at just shy of 14x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates ($3,700/oz gold price assumption in 2026) and at a premium to most of its major producer peers despite a lower growth profile (2025 to 2029), I see this as the least attractive reward/risk setup for Kinross since August 2020, its prior peak ahead of a multi-quarter correction.

Often, when the time comes to trim positions and re-balance, few want to, and while KGC could certainly go higher, I see this as an opportune time to be booking some additional profits after what's been a near parabolic ~2000% rally in the stock (2016 low to 2025 highs), now roughly matching its run from its 2000 bear market lows to 2008 highs.

Q3 Production & Sales

Kinross released its Q3 '25 results earlier this month, reporting quarterly production of ~503,900 gold-equivalent ounces, a 10% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production was related to lower grades at Tasiast, lower grades at Fort Knox (reduced contribution from Manh Choh satellite), and lower production from its Nevada mines, partially offset by higher grades at Paracatu and La Coipa.

Fortunately, Kinross remains well on track to meet guidance despite the softer year-over-year, and this would mark its third consecutive year of beating its guidance midpoint, if achieved, a material improvement from three consecutive misses in the 2020 to 2022 period.

As for its proportion of production from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions, this figure slid to just below 35% vs. 36% in the year-ago period, leaving Kinross as one of the producers with the lowest proportion of production coming from safer and highly regarded Tier-1 jurisdictions. Let's take a look at production by mine:

Quarterly Production by Mine

Fort Knox

Starting with Fort Knox, production came in at ~95,700 ounces at operating costs of $1,359/oz, up from $958/oz in the year-ago period. Lower production year-over-year was attributed to a decline in mill grades (1.86 G/T of gold vs. 4.03 G/T of gold) and lower recoveries, impacted by the ramp-up of Phase 10 ore and lower contribution from the high-grade Manh Choh satellite deposit (only ~286,000 tonnes from Manh Choh in Q3'25). As for cost performance, higher costs were related to fewer ounces sold and higher labor and maintenance supply costs.

On a positive note, Fort Knox is having a solid year despite the planned lower production year-over-year in Q3'25, with ~339,000 ounces produced year-to-date and operating earnings more than doubling year-over-year to ~$475 million in the first nine months of the year (Q1-Q3'24: ~$222 million).

La Coipa

As for its La Coipa Mine, production improved to ~58,000 ounces at costs of $1,199/oz, up from ~50,500 gold-equivalent ounces at $1,074/oz in Q3'24. Higher production was related to higher throughput (~932,000 tonnes) and gold grades (2.36 G/T of gold vs. 2.17 G/T of gold), with operating earnings surging to ~$93 million in Q3'25 vs. ~$34 million in the year-ago period. As for performance vs. guidance, La Coipa will need a strong Q4 to meet its guidance of 230,000 ounces with it sitting at just ~164,500 ounces year-to-date.

As for cost performance, Kinross called out higher royalties, labor and maintenance supply costs, with year-to-date costs sitting at $1,242/oz, nearly $200/oz above its 2025 guidance of $1,060/oz. That said, Kinross deserves a pass here given that cost guidance this year was provided at much lower gold prices with the company budgeting for costs based on a $2,500/oz gold price vs. a gold price that has averaged closer to $3,450/oz year-to-date. Hence, while many producers have missed their cost guidance in 2025, it has largely been because of factors outside their control (primarily increased costs due to higher royalties).

Paracatu

Moving to Kinross' largest mine, Paracatu, production came in at ~150,400 ounces at $933/oz, up from ~146,200 ounces at $1,006/oz in the year-ago period. Higher output was related to increased grades as mining moved into higher-grade ore, partially offset by lower throughput (~13.2 million tonnes vs. ~14.6 million tonnes) due to increased ore hardness that affected mill throughput. Notably, costs were lower year-over-year despite higher royalty costs and contractor costs, and Paracatu is tracking well against guidance of 585,000 ounces with ~80% of planned output delivered year-to-date (~466,300 ounces vs. 585,000 ounces guided).

As for profitability, Paracatu had a phenomenal quarter, generating ~$323 million of operating earnings, with earnings from this asset doubling year-over-year (Q3 '24: ~$159 million). Looking ahead to Q4 '25, we should see another 130,000+ ounce quarter with the benefit of record gold prices, pushing annual operating earnings from this mine upwards of $1.0 billion in 2025.

Tasiast

Finally, Kinross' Tasiast Mine produced ~120,900 ounces at $889/oz costs, down from ~158,500 ounces at $688/oz in the year-ago period. Lower production from this massive African asset (~9MTPA throughput rate) was related to lower throughput and grades (1.78 G/T of gold vs. 2.46 G/T of gold), partially offset by higher recoveries because of optimization work. Lower grades were as expected but significantly affected production and costs, with Tasiast's high-grade West Branch 4 ore blended with low-grade stockpiles.

As it stands, Kinross is heading out of West Branch 4 ore with a focus on development of West Branch 5 to bring high-grade back ore back into the mine plan in the 2027 to 2029 period. Unfortunately, this will weigh on production and costs over the next several quarters when combined with higher royalties, with a 6.5% royalty payable to the Government of Mauritania and a 2.0% royalty payable to Franco-Nevada (FNV).

Given what's likely to be lower production at Tasiast in 2026, Kinross should see much higher costs year-over-year when combined with the headwind of higher royalties. This is because Tasiast is the company's lowest cost operation, with lower contribution from this asset being a minor drag on costs, as well as higher royalties. The good news is that Tasiast has a very bright future ahead, and it should be easier to justify a potential UG component to this mine to fill in the 2030 production profile given what will be a rapid payback if a $3,000/oz+ gold price environment is here to stay.

The latter is similar to what B2Gold has pursued at Fekola, with it able to pick up some of the slack from a grade standpoint with the best years of OP grades behind it, benefiting from ~4.5 G/T Fekola UG ore (70,000+ ounces per year assuming a 1,500 TPD contribution rate).

Financial Results

As for Kinross' financial results, revenue came in at ~$1,802 million (+26% year-over-year), cash flow surged to ~$1,024 million (+40% year-over-year) and attributable free cash flow increased to ~$687 million, translating to ~38% free cash flow margins, a 900+ basis point improvement year-over-year. Given the significant free cash flow generation combined with the sale of its Asante stake (inherited from the Chirano Mine sale), Kinross exited Q3 '25 with ~$1.72 billion in cash and cash equivalents and nearly $500 million in net cash despite ~$165 million spent on share repurchases in Q3 '25 alone.

Looking ahead to Q4 '25, Kinross should enjoy an average realized gold price of $4,100/oz or better, setting up another massive quarter for free cash flow generation with it likely to exit the year with ~$1.0 billion in net cash despite what's expected to be aggressive share repurchases and its recently increased quarterly dividend ($0.035 vs. $0.03), with increased cash flow partially offset by higher capex spend in Q4 '25.

This net cash position will benefit further from the sale of additional Asante shares, with Kinross noting that it exercised its conversion on its 2032 convertible debt into Asante shares and subsequently sold these ~62 million shares for net proceeds of ~$96 million.

As for the bigger picture standpoint and even assuming more conservative gold price assumptions of $3,700/oz next year, Kinross should generate ~$2.2 billion in free cash flow next year, setting the company to exit 2026 with close to $3.0 billion in net cash, assuming only modest share repurchases. This is a great position to be in ahead of a major build later this decade, Great Bear, which could cost closer to $1.6 billion in upfront capex (starting in 2027 and subject to permit timing) adjusted for inflation.

Costs & Margins

Looking at costs and margins, Kinross' all-in sustaining costs came in at $1,622/oz, up 20% year-over-year. Higher AISC was related to increased sustaining capital spend (~$179 million) and the impact of higher royalties and maintenance supply costs at several assets. Fortunately, this was more than offset by the higher gold price with AISC margins climbing to $1,836/oz (~58.1%), up from $1,129/oz (~45.5%) in the year-ago period.

As for Kinross' delivery vs. guidance, FY2025 AISC are sitting at $1,490/oz YTD vs. guidance of $1,500/oz and I wouldn't be surprised to see AISC land above $1,500/oz this year and slightly miss its midpoint. That said, and as noted earlier, this is largely related to higher royalties with solid cost control given that the gold price (XAUUSD:CUR) outperformed its budget by ~$900/oz for estimating costs this year.

As for how margins stacked up with peers, Kinross' AISC margins lagged that of Newmont (NEM), but Kinross had a higher capex quarter from a sustaining capital standpoint vs. Newmont severely underspent on sustaining capex vs. initial plans. Hence, when adjusting for these differences, Kinross' AISC margins were actually among the highest vs. its large-scale producer peers.

Recent Developments

Curlew

As for its Curlew Project in Washington, Kinross noted that initial development of the Roadrunner exploration decline is complete, as well as North Stealth development, allowing for better drill access of the high-grade Roadrunner target and potential extensions to mineralization at North Stealth. This asset could see a production decision approved next year, with Curlew potentially supporting a ~100,000 ounce production profile and helping to offset what's likely to be a lower production profile post-2029 at Tasiast.

Great Bear

Moving to its cornerstone development project, Great Bear, this asset in Ontario, Canada will be a game-changer for the company with the ability to bring online ~500,000 high-margin gold ounces per annum at industry-leading AISC below $950/oz (*) in its first eight years of higher production (~518,000 ounces). Kinross spent ~$28 million at Great Bear in Q3 '25 and has spent over ~$76 million year-to-date, noting that its Main Project remains on schedule with detailed engineering for the mill, TMF and other site infrastructure underway.

(*) Kinross' LOM AISC estimate of $812/oz was based on a PEA, which generally understates operating costs, and a gold price of $1,900/oz, with a $40/oz impact from royalties alone at $3,900/oz gold. (*)

Unfortunately, while the natural gas pipeline has been commissioned and its camp is operational as part of its AEX (Advanced Exploration) program, the company has only received three of five permits (two water permits remain outstanding), with it previously noting that it hoped to receive this before year-end. This has resulted in a minor slip in the schedule for the AEX program, with the excavation of the exploration decline not expected to start until summer 2026.

Although this is a minor disappointment, the goal of the AEX program is to extract a bulk sample and complete additional definition/infill drilling of UG material in the coming years. Importantly, this minor slip in schedule because of delayed permits does not impede the start of the main project. This is because the bulk of material fed to the future ~3.7 MTPA mill is open-pit material in the first few years, including a portion of 2029 feed from stockpiles/open-pit material.

Share Repurchases

As for deploying cash from its record free cash flow generation, Kinross has shared that besides redeeming its $500 million in 2027 Senior Notes in December, it had repurchased ~$405 million in shares up to early November vs. no repurchases completed in 2023 and 2024. This included ~8.8 million shares repurchased in Q3 '25 alone, and the company has upped its buyback program to $600 million minimum for 2025, suggesting another ~$195 million in share repurchases in the last seven weeks of the year.

Although this is certainly one way to return capital to shareholders vs. increasing the base dividend further or paying a special dividend, I'm a little surprised at the level of buybacks which seem to be more predicated on its financial position than the valuation of the stock. Personally, I prefer those companies that repurchase shares opportunistically to those that are buying back shares with less attention paid to valuations and are not price sensitive, but obviously the market sees things differently than me given how well KGC has performed.

To be clear, KGC is not expensive at over 14x FY2026 free cash flow estimates ($3,700/oz gold), but it's not cheap either and I would have much preferred to this level of aggressive buybacks below US$8.00/share vs. waiting for a 600% rally in the stock (US$3.00 ----> US$21.00+) to deploy record sums of capital into these share buybacks.

Valuation

Based on ~1.21 billion shares and a share price of US$28.20, Kinross trades at a market cap of ~$34.1 billion and an enterprise value of ~$33.6 billion. This makes Kinross one of the most expensive producers of its scale in the market today on a valuation per ounce produced basis compared to other peers with less than 50% of production coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions.

In fact, Kinross now trades at over $17,000 per ounce of 2026 gold production, significantly above one of its closest peers by scale, Northern Star (OTCPK:NESRF), at ~$14,000/oz. This is despite Northern Star being as Tier-1 only jurisdiction producer with far stronger growth rates, which should leave it trading at a premium to Kinross. Meanwhile, Kinross trades at more than double the 2026 free cash flow of B2Gold (BTG) and nearly three times its value on a market cap per ounce produced basis, with B2Gold trading at barely ~$6,000 per ounce of 2026 gold production.

So, from purely a relative value standpoint, Kinross has rarely ever been less attractive at current levels. Further, if we look at where the stock has traded historically, Kinross has typically peaked near cash flow over the past decade. Today, Kinross now finds itself trading back at over ~9x cash flow using a base case assumption of $3,700/oz gold price assumption for 2026, meaning it is far more gold price reliant than peers given it was already seen considerable multiple expansion from when it was buried below 3.5x cash flow at dirt-cheap levels in 2022.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 1.1x P/NAV (7% discount rate) and 8.5x FY2026 cash flow estimates using base case assumptions ($3,700/oz gold), I see KGC's updated fair value coming in at US$24.00/share. If we use less conservative gold price assumptions of $4,100/oz in 2026, Kinross' fair value increases to US$25.60, still leaving it fully valued at current levels.

Technical Picture

Finally, as for its technical picture, Kinross continues to have one of the least attractive technical setups sector wide, rallying into an area that has been strong resistance for the past thirty years while sporting its most significant level of over-extension since 2008 (its prior peak). This doesn't mean that the stock can't go higher and take out this resistance level, but from purely a reward/risk standpoint, it's difficult to find a less attractive technical setup in the sector today.

Hence, I don't see any way to justify chasing the stock here and continue to see far more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector. Further, if I were still long the stock, I would be using this rally to take more profits into strength above US$28.20.

Summary

Kinross put together another solid quarter in Q3 '25 and remains well on track to meet its 2025 guidance of 2.0 million gold-equivalent ounces. Just as importantly, the company has done a decent job of controlling costs. Further, while holding the 2.0 million ounce per annum mark until 2030 when Great Bear is expected to move into commercial production looked like a tall task previously, higher gold prices have materially improved that outlook with it much easier to justify projects like Redbird Phase 2 and Curlew in a $3,000/oz+ gold price environment. Hence, investors need not worry about a production cliff later this decade prior to bringing Great Bear online.

While these are all positive developments, an ~800% rally in three years has left these positives priced into the stock and Kinross has rarely ever been more expensive relative to its peers. Assuming Kinross were a Tier-1 only producer with a strong growth profile and a solid track record of per share growth, a cash flow multiple closer to 9 might make more sense. However, Kinross has one of the weaker per share growth records sector-wide, one of the weakest growth profiles sector-wide (2025 --> 2029), and I'd expect to see much higher operating costs year-over-year with reduced contribution from its lowest-cost Tasiast Mine.

Obviously, others' views on fair value and my view of fair value could differ materially, and I have clearly underestimated the strength of KGC's rally to date. That said, I struggle to justify paying a mid double-digit forward free cash flow multiple (~14x) for a depleting business with over 50% of production coming from Tier-2 ranked jurisdictions.

So, while I think Kinross' leadership has done a great job turning the business around and we've seen a return to improved capital discipline vs. the prior cycle (expensive/failed acquisitions that torpedoed per share metrics in Red Back/Aurelian), I simply see it as a solid business at a full price today.

data.title: BNP Paribas: Solid Quarter, Higher Targets - But Sudan Overhang Keeps Us Neutral
data.description:
Our timing in downgrading BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQF)(OTCQX:BNPQY) proved fortunate (Fig. 1). Just a few days later, the bank's shares came under pressure amid news of the Sudan case and rising concerns over legal claims.

In our latest update, we recognise several headwinds, including ongoing French politics noise and limited visibility into the year-end budget vote, likely to crystallise over the coming quarters. This is also combined with a Global Markets provisions booked in Q3, which have raised fresh questions on asset quality.

Fig 1

Sudan Case and Q3 Results

In the BNP press release, the company reaffirmed "its unwavering intention to appeal" the ruling, and said it was "going to pursue all available avenues to contest this judgment." And the company believes that "this verdict is specific to these three plaintiffs and should not have broader application." This brings us back memories of the French bank, which paid almost €9 billion in fines in 2014.

We will keep our remarks on Q3 concise, given the additional update and the already strong quarterly results. YTD top-line sales reached €38 billion with a 3.9% result, but in Q3, BNP turnover accelerated by 5.3% (Fig. 2). Part of this growth is explained by the bank's Investment & Protection Services. Sales were up by +27.5% due to AXA IM consolidation. The bank's operating expenses were slightly higher than anticipated, reflecting the exceptional impact of restructuring costs and AXA IM adaptation costs. These costs totalled €154 million. The company is well capitalised with a coverage ratio of 138%. Cost of risks was aligned with H1 results, and BNP Paribas Common Equity Tier 1 reached 12.5%. For this reason, the company confirmed its 2026 outlook trajectory with sales above 5%, cost of risks below 40 basis points, and EPS CAGR above 8%.

Fig 2

BNP's New Target and Our Neutral View

While the Sudan litigation is likely to weigh on the equity story for a while yet, BNP Paribas is trading at an 18% cost of equity. This is punitive; however, BNP Paribas' priority remains faster capital build, both organically and through portfolio moves. At this stage, BNP shares are trading at a 2026 P/E of 6.1x and a 0.78x P/TBV, with a Return on Tangible Equity above 11%. Even if the yield is 10%, the bank might look mispriced - this litigation might weigh on BNP shares.

On 20 November, BNP Paribas communicated a higher target (ahead of the Q3 guidance reiteration on the 2024-2026 plan). So, we report 1) a 13% Common Equity Tier 1 target compared to a previous 12.5%), 2) disposal of non core assets (this might also help alleviate the Sudan potential litigation), 3) a cost income ratio of 61% in 2026 and 58% moving into the 2028, and 4) a commitment to return excess capital to investor. The bank also announced a €1.15 billion share buyback. The ECB already authorises this and provides relief in the litigation case. While we previously highlighted that BNP was trailing peers with more modest targets, the bank is now guiding to stronger growth, marking a clear upgrade versus our last update.

In Q3, deposit competition eased, and the bank reported net interest income amid steepening yields. Loan growth and Arval margins are finally stabilising. With the AXA IMI acquisition, the group is now a leader in asset management (Fig. 3) and will be less reliant on rates and more on fee generation.

Fig 3

Earnings Changes And Valuation

BNP Paribas will now benefit from 1) a higher-for-longer interest rate environment, and 2) higher asset management fee generation with no relation to rates. There is also a clear indication to reduce the cost/income ratio, and on the cost of risk, the group maintained its 40-basis-point forward outlook. Compared to our previous estimates, we believe BNP could achieve higher profits and EPS (thanks to the buyback execution), implying a result of €10.95. This assumes at least a 50-basis-point rate cut from the ECB in 2026. Additionally, the bank's tangible book value per share was €91.2 (Fig. 4). With no change to our valuation methodology (6.5x P/E target and 0.8x P/TBV), we arrived at a value of €72 per share. With BNP Paribas shares trading at €70 per share ($41.5 in ADR), we still believe a neutral rating is justified.

Fig 4

Risks

BNP downside risks include this new litigation in Sudan, and given the potential appeal, litigation costs, and outcome, we believe shares will be volatile on news developments. The bank risks include lower rates, higher regulatory costs, lower CIB sales, and fee income from volatile equity markets. We see downside from France's political uncertainties, combined with potentially higher taxation. Additionally, risks include operational risk, cybersecurity failure, wage inflation, and operating costs.

Conclusion

It was a good quarter for BNP Paribas, and we see a positive evolution following the higher guidance and buyback on 20 November. That said, from a valuation standpoint, BNP Paribas still looks pricey, and as already reported, the Sudan claims will likely remain an overhang on the stock price.

data.title: The S&P 500 Put The 'V' In November
data.description:
Stocks put the “V” in November. The S&P 500 (SP500) finished about flat for the month, which is an impressive feat, as the US large-cap index was –4.3% for the period just a week ago. The Nasdaq 100 (NDX) underperformed, losing 2%, while the Russell 2000 (RTY) posted a percentage point of alpha. Elsewhere, mid-caps scaled 2%.

Overseas stocks fared fine. The Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU) was fractionally green as the US Dollar Index (DXY) wobbled around the 100 mark. Over in the bond market, the aggregate fixed income index returned just a bit under 1%.

In commodities, oil (CL1:COM) gave back 2%, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) soared 5%, and bitcoin (BTC-USD) plunged 17%. The entire crypto space stormed back over November’s final week, though. Bitcoin neared $80,000 at the low, only to reach $92,000 by Black Friday afternoon. Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) notched record highs on Black Friday.

November Returns: Gold Best, Bitcoin Worst, SPY Flat

Putting the "V" in November

Digging into the sector view, Health Care once again led. The pharma-heavy area of the S&P 500 jumped 9%, double the performance of the next best sectors (Materials and Consumer Staples).

Lagging was Information Technology. Nvidia (NVDA) dragged on the group, while shares of Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL) left something to be desired. To be fair, AAPL notched fresh record highs over the month’s final handful of sessions.

A standout tech stock was Broadcom (AVGO), which rose to new highs of its own. Oracle (ORCL), however, continued to sour, with shares giving back 23%.

S&P 500 Sector ETF Performances: Health Care Led in November, Tech Lagged

S&P 500 Performance Heat Map

Turning to the economy, the now-stale September jobs report revealed a fresh multi-year high in the unemployment rate, despite a better-than-expected headline payrolls gain. We should get the November employment data on time next Friday morning, but we may not know the precise state of the labor market for October.

Investors should also pay attention to private-sector jobs clues via next week’s PMI Employment subindexes, the JOLTS report, ADP Private Payrolls, jobless claims, and the November Challenger Job Cuts.

Unemployment Rate Rises to the Highest Since October 2021

The consumer is, of course, in focus on this Black Friday. Early indicators point to intense spending, even with higher price tags on the store shelves.

Once again, we don’t have the latest Retail Sales numbers from the US Census Bureau. We have in hand, however, the weekly Johnson Redbook retail sales data, and it shows a solid 5.9% YoY rise in total spending. Thanksgiving-Black Friday consumer trends point to even better gains.

Johnson Redbook +5.9% YoY

Early Black Friday Sales Robust

As for inflation, the September CPI report confirmed what macro onlookers already knew—the prevailing consumer-price rise is about 3% over the past 12 months. But US home values appear to be flat to in decline in some key regions. Hence, real-time inflation data (using timely shelter price data) point to inflation closer to 2.2%.

Back out tariffs (which are seen by some as transitory), and broader inflation appears tame. Just take a look at the inflation swaps market—traders price in 2.5% inflation across timeframes.

US CPI Holds Near 3%

Inflation Swaps Now Centered on 2.5%

Putting it all together, Q3 US real GDP growth appears to be headed for a 3.5% print (or better). Early looks at Q4 expansion are also sanguine.

All of this comes ahead of what should be a big tax-refund season, given the effects of President Trump’s One Big, Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA). Expect spending to tick up beginning in February.

US Real GDP Growth Seen Near 3.7% For Q3

The lynchpin to the holiday-week rally to close out November was a dovish turn regarding Fed speak. NY Fed President John Williams cracked the door wide open to a December rate cut on Friday, November 21--the S&P 500 jumped and never looked back into the month-end.

The FOMC enters its blackout period tonight, and it appears likely that a quarter-point ease is in the cards come December 10.

A December Rate Cut Likely

Wrapping things up with the S&P 500 earnings trends, we see more optimistic data. Both 2025 and 2026 EPS estimates are now at multi-month highs.

That’s a somewhat unusual bullish trend, as earnings forecasts are often trimmed as the calendar year wears on. In fact, S&P 500 EPS is now above $300 on a forward-12-month basis for the first time.

S&P 500 EPS Estimates Rising

S&P 500 Forward EPS $306

December Often Is A Strong Month

December Returns...Back-Half Loaded, On Average

The Bottom Line

Stocks battled back to the flat line in November, led not by tech but by many blue chips. December has historically been a strong month, but gains are often back-half loaded. All eyes will be on the Fed on December 10, as well as tariff news and year-ahead outlooks.

data.title: Freehold Royalties Takes Texas Pacific Land To The Cleaners
data.description:
It has been almost a year since we penned this piece.

Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) has in the interim delivered almost exactly 35% down versus the 70% slide we were looking for.

Whoa, we're half way there

Whoa oh, livin' on a prayer

Hit the bid, we'll make it I swear

Whoa oh, livin' on a prayer

Source: Livin On A Prayer (Bear Edition)

In that piece and the sequel, we told investors just two things.

1) Avoid TPL, like the bubonic plague.

2) If you want royalty exposure, buy Freehold Royalties (FRU:CA). This long-short trade made you 52% over a year.

We review the recent results, valuation and tell you why we would look for a swing trade in TPL after year-end tax loss selling. We also go over our outlook for Freehold.

Q3-2025

TPL missed estimates by a mile and came in 50 cents below where the analyst community was aiming. The analyst community in this case is just one single, number cruncher, who probably has the least enviable task of figuring out production and commodity prices in advance

We are unsure as well as to what would have been a reasonable expectation but TPL did manage to deliver some goodies to the bulls. Production was up nicely across all 3 commodities and even realized pricing improved from a year ago. They key positive metric though was the quarter over quarter improvement, where production was up sharply.

One area which was a bit off though, was the surge in water-related expenses. But this has demonstrated a fair bit of variability over the last few years. Equally importantly, the year to date number for this expense wasn't particularly high. Management addressed the growth in the water division during the conference call.

Turning to our Water Services and Operations segment. This business rebounded considerably from last quarter. As I mentioned earlier, water sales revenue was a record for third quarter 2025. Although rigs and frac spreads have trended lower, upstream operators continue to prioritize development efficiencies as we see persistent deployment of co-completions and simul and trimul fracking. Our investments in brackish and treated water infrastructure have established TPL as one of the few systems in the Permian capable of accommodating the volume intensity required to keep up with operators.

On the produced water royalty side, revenues and volumes continue to perform well. Year-over-year, quarterly revenues and volumes were up 16% and 19%, respectively, as we see strong demand for both in-basin and out-of-basin pore space.

Source: TPL Q3-2025 Conference Call Transcript

Overall it was not a bad quarter, though it appeared like TPL missed.

TPL Tells You What It Is Worth

We've gotta hold on to what we've bought

It doesn't make a difference if they build a data center or not

We've got the water business and that's a lot for, 27X sales,

We'll give it a shot

Source: Livin On A Prayer (Bull Version)

TPL decided to address the "bubble" talk indirectly. They bought some new acreage.

To that end, yesterday we announced acquisitions of Permian oil and gas royalties and surface acreage which fits seamlessly into the broader TPL portfolio. On November 3, 2025, we acquired approximately 17,300 net royalty acres standardized to 1/8 located primarily in the Midland Basin in Martin, Howard and Midland Counties.

Total purchase price was approximately $474 million, funded entirely by cash on our balance sheet. Approximately 70% of the acquired interests are adjacent to or overlapping drilling spacing units that TPL already owns, meaning we essentially acquired additional royalties in current and future well locations we already retain an interest in. Approximately 61% of the royalty acreage is operated by Exxon, Diamondback and Occidental. The royalty acquisition currently produces more than 3,700 barrels of oil equivalent per day with an approximately 80% oil and natural gas liquids cut.

Source: TPL Q3-2025 Conference Call Transcript (emphasis ours)

The math is simple. TPL produces 36,300 barrels of oil equivalent a day. They just bought about one-tenth of that production for $474 million. Ergo, and hear us out before you cover your ears (we are looking at you who went all in at $1,700 a share), the total value of the company is about $4.74 billion. In stark contrast, here is where TPL trades.

You can see why we used every expletive (privately, SA has standards) while describing the company when it traded at $36 billion. Getting back to the current valuation, it still appears extremely high. It still appears extremely high if you build in doubling of oil and gas prices.

TPL Vs Freehold Royalties

Those that took our recommendation for switching to Freehold, we have some good news. The good news is that TPL still looks like a giant pile of junk relative to Freehold. There really is no reason to pay 18X extra on a sales multiple unless you are a real masochist looking for tax losses.

There is no reason to buy TPL based on the dividend yield either...unless you are indifferent to 0.74% and 7.22% dividend yield.

Our fair value for TPL is less than half the current price, but even if you go by the only analyst on Wall Street that has given an opinion, TPL trades at a 55% premium to NAV.

In contrast, Freehold is at a 9% discount.

Of course, the market could once again ignore valuation and give TPL investors a little extra hope. This is exactly what happened last year as there were a few rallies driven by news of data center buildouts on TPL land. That of course made zero sense as most of land owned by TPL is in extremely hostile environmental territory. Anyone considering building a data center there needs to have their head examined. But even leaving that lack of mental acuity aside, TPL owns less than 1% of the total acreage in Texas. So mathematically, if 100 data centers were built in Texas, one or two might be on TPL property. Oil and gas producing land commands some premium as shown in the recent acquisition price. Barren/grazing land is so cheap that we would assign 1-5% of total TPL NAV to that portion of their assets. Selling some to build a data center won't even pay the CEO's salary for the year. We think that the longer term path for TPL is firmly down, though the stock might stage a rebound post tax-loss selling. The stock is 21.3% below its 200 day moving average, and that has historically produced some incredible rallies.

So we are hesitant to put a "Strong Sell" on this at these levels. We would be happy to do so once it rises up enough to meet the 200 day moving average.

On a different note, we are moving Freehold to a "hold", as the Canadian energy stocks have just crushed their US brethren. We are finding incredible value deals in US energy (on a relative basis) for the first time in ages. Just not in TPL.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Why Oil Continues To Slump While Other Commodities Rally
data.description:
Oil may be down 13% this year, but commodities as a whole are climbing. Hussein Allidina, Managing Director and Head of Commodities at TD Asset Management, MoneyTalk to unpack the unusual split, why crude could be facing further declines and what investors should watch heading into 2026.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: Commodities have been full of surprises this year. Oil prices are down about 13% year to date. Yet, a quick look at the space overall-- when you include commodities like gold, copper, natural gas, and others-- you'll see the sector is up by roughly the same amount. My next guest says that's because there's a lot going on beneath the surface. So as we head into 2026, what does that mean for the outlook for prices and portfolio construction?

Joining me now, Hussein Allidina, head of commodities at TD Asset Management. It is lovely to have you here.

Hussein Allidina: Thanks for having me.

Kim Parlee: Let's start with the chart, because that is going to illustrate. That's the reason I talked about what I did when I started this conversation. Let's bring it up, and you tell me what we're looking at here.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so this is a time series of the Bloomberg Commodity Index. That's the benchmark of the strategy that we manage. And it includes over 20 commodities. One-third of the basket is energy, so your crude, natural gas, gasoline, diesel, et cetera. One-third of the basket, roughly, is base and precious metals. And one-third is grain, softs and livestock. So it's a diversified basket of commodity exposure.

Generally, that's the benchmark that folks are looking at when they're thinking about portfolio construction and introducing commodities into their asset mix. When you read academic research, which is a ton of fun, nine times out of 10-- nine times out of 10, they're referring to this benchmark when they're talking about why one should have a commodity allocation in a diversified, balanced portfolio.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, well, up and to the right is always a good thing-- which it was.

Hussein Allidina: Well, look. I think this is interesting, because when I speak with clients, they're like, well, oil's off 15% year to date-- which is, to be clear, that's the spot price of oil, which is very different than if you were long oil and rolling futures. That return this year is only down 6% because the curve is in backwardation. But the key here is that commodities are not homogeneous. They all have different supply-demand balances.

What I think is homogeneous, though, for the commodities space is that capital has not been attracted into this space for the better part of 10-12 years. So we're starting to see the supply side across commodities tighten. There are exceptions. Today oil, I think, is oversupplied. But generally speaking, we've starved the space of capital. And, as a result, inventories are tightening because we continue to consume this stuff.

Kim Parlee: So I want to spend the first part talking about commodities within the portfolio. Second part, we'll talk about individual commodities. Tell me more about the starving of capital into the commodities space. And maybe how does that compare to previous periods and what you see?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so, commodities. So we have a ton of recency bias. Commodities, I guess, pre-2020 spent 10 years in this, what I call, exploitation phase. We were exploiting the investment that we made in the super-cycle which started in the early 2000s. The increase in price that we saw in the early 2000s brought about a tremendous amount of capital. We've joked about this before-- it's not Amazon Prime. It takes time before that capital that you've put into the ground lends to an increase in oil production, copper production.

Kim Parlee: Long time.

Hussein Allidina: Five, 10 years. So for the last 10, 15 years, we've been exploiting it because we made all that investment. And capital has not gone into the commodities space-- which is the right thing, because prices were weak and balances were oversupplied. Today, if we look at where capital is going, it's going into Mag 7, AI, data centers, hyperscalers. Those functions are very, very commodity-intensive. You need a lot of power to drive the language models. You need a lot of copper to move that power.

And unlike prior cycles-- we talked about the cycle which started in the '70s-- capital prior to that was going into the Nifty 50, the Kodak Polaroid. In the cycle that started in the 2000s, the period before that saw capital going into the internet stocks-- Pets.com, Mozilla, Netscape, et cetera. Those were not very commodity-intensive places. Today, capital is going into a space that's very commodity-intensive, and that space is taking capital away-- if capital is fixed-- away from commodities.

Commodities have started to work. Commodity returns have averaged north of 10% annualized since 2020. But that's still not as meaningful as the returns folks are generating in the AI space. And my concern is that where I'm spending money today is actually very, very commodity-intensive, and I'm not spending sufficient capital to grow my commodity supply base.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, and I think that's the interesting, I guess, conundrum when you're saying-- or not conundrum, opportunity from your perspective-- is that we're saying that AI needs lots of commodities, yet the commodities space has been starved of capital because AI's been sucking up all the capital. And so, it's almost this double reinforcing piece that you see where things could go.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah. So I think commodity prices and commodity returns would be positive and above average over the course of the next decade, irrespective of the AI, just because we've starved it of capital. And GDP continues to grow, so our consumption globally of commodities is growing. Now I have this additional fillip where I'm actually creating a new source of demand, and it's meaningful. So, I think the setup is actually quite interesting. And I don't think portfolios, Kim, are constructed to benefit from this new reality.

Kim Parlee: And you were saying that just before we get to the individuals. But that's not just specific, I'll say, of gold-- because we were talking about that-- and also to general commodities. What do you see in portfolios, versus what do you think should be true for portfolios?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so if I look at the last-- if I look at the 20 years into COVID, I would argue that period of depressed inflation, very low rates was the anomaly. If I look at 100 years of data, that 20 years into COVID, or really the 15 years post the financial crisis and the QE and financial repression, led to very weak rates, very low inflation. And the optimal portfolio for that environment is a portfolio of fixed income and equities, right?

You don't really need diversification because when inflation is low, fixed income and equities have a negative correlation. So you get your diversification there. You're not looking for inflation protection because there was no inflation. I don't think that we necessarily are in an environment where inflation is going to average the 5%, 6%, 7% that we saw after COVID. But I don't think it's a stretch to say that the 20 years into COVID was the anomaly. And it's likely that the volatility around both growth and inflation is going to be higher in the next 10 years.

And that portfolio-- if you're designing that portfolio-- it includes infrastructure, it includes real estate, it includes commodities, because you have to hedge those other risks that we didn't really face in that sort of pre-COVID period.

Kim Parlee: Let's start with oil. I know we said it's down while the rest of the complex is up. But why is it down?

Hussein Allidina: The balance is in surplus. So we, throughout the year, we're probably more conservative-- less bearish than consensus. Last year, got some weak data out of China, and the market writ off the idea of demand growth. Demand is growing. The world is going to see probably 900 to 1 million barrels a day of demand growth this year. But you have non-OPEC production increasing on the heels of the high prices that we saw in 2011-2012 and, to a lesser extent, some of the price appreciation we saw in 2022.

So, longer lead time projects in Brazil, Guyana, and Canada are coming to fruition. But at the same time, we do have Permian production and shale production starting to flatten and decline. OPEC is adding barrels back to the market. On paper, we have a surplus of somewhere between 2 and 4 million barrels a day, which is big. We're going to see big inventory builds, and that should pressure prices over the course of the next three to six months.

There's risk to this, Kim, of course. We have continuing geopolitics in the Middle East, continuing geopolitics in Russia, Ukraine, increased activity in the Caribbean. So there is risk, but on paper, your supply and demand looks bearish because of this increase in production. So we think that over the course of the near term, there is downside. We think you could trade down into the low 50s.

And ultimately, that does discourage the producers from deploying capital and we create an issue down the road. But today's focus is very much on this oversupplied balance. Inventories are building on the water, so you've seen a tremendous increase of oil on the water. At some point, that makes its way into onshore inventories and challenges the price and the structure, is our base case.

Kim Parlee: What about gold? And gold has had an incredible rally in the past little while. So, how much longer?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, look, I think structurally, the case for gold is very much this emerging market central bank buying that I'm probably putting you to death on, we've talked about it so many times. But emerging markets hold more aggregate reserves than developed markets. So I'm talking paper and gold in aggregate.

But when I look at their asset allocation, they're sitting in a lot of paper. The weighted average gold holding for the developed market central bank is around 60% of assets. The number for the emerging market is 10%, and this is after they've bought over 1,000 tons a year for the last three years, and after the price appreciation--

Kim Parlee: That's fascinating.

Hussein Allidina: So will those emerging market central banks stop buying gold? They might pause when you see a big rally in price. But it's hard for me to believe that directionally, the macro supports wanting to hold more dollars when you already have 90% of your reserves in dollars. So I think you'll continue to see that diversification that will continue to support gold. And I think you'll see retail institutional interest in gold that is a reflection of concerns around debasement, or Fed independence, or the performance of equities.

It's remarkable, actually, that gold has done what it's done this year--

Kim Parlee: Absolutely.

Hussein Allidina: - --notwithstanding the fact that equities have continued to perform.

Kim Parlee: Fascinating times. We've only got about three minutes. I'm trying to get this in, but critical minerals. And I know some of these are rare earths, critical minerals. This is not something that you trade and hold and those types of things. But what's been interesting, as you mentioned, is what's now been declared a critical mineral.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, yeah, so the US last week or the week before came out with an updated list on what's critical. Copper and nickel alloy are there. And I think this really just underscores the importance of commodities in making the world work.

Kim Parlee: National security.

Hussein Allidina: Well, and so the US has recognized that a lot of the things that we want to do requires these commodities. A lot of copper to build out the power generation, which has been underinvested in even before this sort of AI theme came to fruition. So I think this is an effort to underscore the importance of these commodities and to encourage domestic production.

Kim Parlee: Can I ask you about silver? I mean, it tends to track gold, but I know it has an industrial use as well, too.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, silver definitely has more of an industrial component than gold. We have invested throughout the year in silver from both the long and short side. We don't share the same sentiment to silver that we do to gold because we don't think that central banks are going to start buying silver. It's far more-- it's larger to store. So it doesn't have that benefit, but it does trade and will continue to trade, probably, with gold-- just a more volatile version.

Kim Parlee: I don't know if we're out of time, but we will. What about the grains and the soft commodities? What's your outlook for that?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so the grains broadly-- so grains and softs different. Grains have found quite a bit of price appreciation over the course of the last several weeks on anticipation of improved trade relations between China and the US. I think President Trump has probably tweeted specifically about soybeans more than a handful of times. When we look at the fundamental balances, China has been buying a lot of beans from Brazil, from Argentina over the course of the year.

They may buy some more beans from the US, I think, to appease President Trump. But they're not taking 20 million tons, we don't think, this year. If we look at crush margins in China, if we look at the size of the hog herd in China, it's all contracting, and they're sitting on a pretty good quantity of beans. So we think that rally in grains is probably one to be sold.

When we look at conditions in South America, Brazil, Argentina, estimates on their crops continue to get larger. I think there's been, again, a little bit of a relief rally on the heels of what might be more volume going from the US to China, but the data doesn't support it.

Kim Parlee: I've only got literally 10 seconds. This is unfair, but weather. Weather is so much more volatile than it used to be. How does that play into how you see things?

Hussein Allidina: It plays a meaningful role in natural gas, in particular. So, we like today owning European gas because we think it's not pricing any weather risk. The start of the weather has been-- the winter has been relatively mild, which is tempering things. We have a lot of winter ahead of us. Weather plays a role into our fundamental analysis. We assume normal weather. We will play on either side of that to see what happens. But ultimately, weather does play a role.
